topic: Two friends realize they remember a shared event completely differently
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee cup rattled in Elias's hand, a sharp, metallic sound against the worn wooden table. He stared at Maya, his jaw tight enough to crack stone. "You were the one who tripped," he stated, the words clipped and low, his voice vibrating with undisguised frustration. He leaned forward, his knuckles white where he gripped the edge of the table. "I distinctly recall you stumbling right before the presentation started. You nearly knocked over the entire projector." Maya blinked, a slow, confused movement that didn't quite reach her eyes. "What? Elias, that’s not what happened. I remember *you* fumbling with the remote, completely messing up the slide transitions." Elias let out a short, harsh exhale, pushing his chair back with a scrape that echoed in the sudden silence. He ran a hand over his face, the skin hot beneath his palm. "No. That’s not right. You’re twisting things. I saw you."

[story 2]

"It doesn't matter what you *think* you saw," Liam snapped, his voice pitched higher than usual, laced with a brittle edge. He paced the cramped apartment floor, his movements jerky and agitated. He stopped abruptly, planting his hands on his hips, his shoulders hiked up toward his ears. "I was there, Maya. I was standing right behind you when the vase fell. You were laughing, completely unbothered, while I was trying to catch it. You said, 'It’s fine, just a little clumsy.'" Maya shifted uncomfortably, her gaze darting around the room as if searching for an escape route. "That’s absurd, Liam. I remember you rushing in, trying to grab the centerpiece, and you knocked it over completely. I saw your face; you looked mortified." Liam’s breath hitched, a sharp intake of air that sounded like a suppressed growl. He clenched his fists until his nails dug crescents into his palms. "Stop trying to rewrite the timeline. I felt the impact. I *know* what I saw."

[story 3]

Sarah slammed the textbook shut, the sound sharp enough to make the nearby students jump. Her eyes, narrowed and dark, fixed on Ben. "Don't play dumb with me," she hissed, her tone dangerously level, every syllable weighted with disapproval. She crossed her arms tightly across her chest, pulling her shoulders inward as if bracing for a blow. "I was the one who tripped over the loose cable. I remember the whole sequence—the flash of light, my own unsteady feet. You were standing there, watching, completely still." Ben rubbed the back of his neck, a nervous habit that only seemed to amplify the tension radiating off him. "That’s not accurate," he countered, his voice strained. "I distinctly recall you lunging forward, trying to pull the cord yourself, and you nearly took out the entire power strip. I saw your frantic movements." Sarah’s lips pressed into a thin, hard line. She took a deliberate step closer, invading his space, her gaze unwavering. "I felt the shockwave. You are deliberately misremembering."
